And so I'm really excited to get into a lot of that work and about Shipstead. You But before we get started into all of that, you wear a lot of different hats at Shipstead. And so can you give us an overview for our listeners about what Shipstead is, which I think is one of the most innovative media companies, and how your role at Shipstead covers a lot of these different aspects of work that's going on in AI. Absolutely. So Schivstad Media is a Nordic media group operating the major news destinations in Norway and Sweden. We also run a number of regional and local newspapers in the same markets. We've been around since the 1800s and we've kind of evolved through various innovations and acquisitions that led to us becoming a big ecosystem of digital services, which were actually just recently spun off. So Schipstead was part of a a bigger group on the stock exchange in Oslo, but was just bought in full by the Tilnius Trust. So we are now a dedicated media company uh owned by a trust, the Tinius Trust, working to promote both our brands, but Independent journalism in and beyond the Nordics. My role at Chip said, I've uh had a lot of different roles in the past six years with a company. And now I have one that, as you said, covers quite a lot of different areas. I have a lot of words in my title, but none of them are actually AI, which I think is rare right now. But uh my title is Head of InLab and Trust Initiatives. And what that means is that I, on the one hand, head up a inclusion and innovation unit called IMLab, short for inclusion lab. And I'm sure sure we'll get into what we do later in this conversation. And I also run trust initiatives, meaning that I gather a group of senior leaders from across Shipset to work collectively on issues related to media trust. In addition to my work at Chipstead or as part of my work at Chipstead, I'm also an industrial PhD candidate. So I'm writing a doctoral dissertation on AI and journalism and I have been for the past four years.

chunks.document: 2024-11-27 - Agnes Stenbom Reimagining News Through the Eyes of Gen Z.txt
chunks.text: And as you said, your title does not include AI anywhere, but All of your work just shows how much AI is not something that should be siloed away in the newsroom and it in fact is related to you. trust and prototyping what the news features look like, the news future looks like. So I was part of founding IMLAB back in 2021 and I did that coming from a background working with AI product development in Shipset. And At that time, we were doing quite a lot of exciting things, working with AI to, I'd say, enhance the products for people who were already on the inside. We were doing lots of great things for those who were already committed to us and to journalism. But we could see at that time and today as well, an escalating trend of news avoidance and information fragmentation that really worried me. And we could see certain groups in society not connecting to journalism or with journalism, with in our markets around 30% actively choosing to avoid us, to not consume us, often due to low trust and feelings of our product being a negative force in their lives. And I worried and I still worry quite a lot around what that will mean in the long term for this group's democratic participation, especially when you look at the fact That news avoiders are typically people who are already marginalized in society in different ways. If you have a relatively low uh socioeconomic status or capital, that makes you a more likely news avoider, for example. So I was very worried about that trend, but I was at the same time very excited about the fact that we had all of these possible technological tools that we were not exploring for these groups specifically So I wanted to see what would happen if we paired the trend of news avoidance and information fragmentation with the rapid technological growth that we could see, not least within the AI domain. Yeah, so my job before founding INLAB was to lead responsible AI development at Shipsted. So one of the things that We did was to develop a framework called FAST. FAST is an acronym for fairness, accountability, sustainability, and transparency. And it's a risk assessment framework building on awareness around what the risks in those particular areas are and how you as a product development team can meet those risks. So we built a structure starting with trainings, making sure that that people involved in AI development always knew about these risks, these areas. So kind of starting with awareness and then resources, workshop materials, questions for teams to consider. educational materials, uh like research articles to to explore and uh testing tools to to test their product on, etc. Just a bank of resources for teams to test their own product. chunks.text: This is Newsroom Robots, the podcast where we explore the intersection of artificial intelligence and the news industry. I'm Nikita Roy, data scientist, media entrepreneur, and one of the many founders currently building their. Ventures at the Harvard Innovation Labs. On the Newsroom Robots, I'm excited to bring you insightful conversations with industry experts about how AI is impacting the way we do journalism. Joining me on the show today is Opasna Gotham. She's a senior product manager at CNN where she leads the development of their content management system that delivers news to millions globally. She also serves as the board chair at the News Product Alliance, working to elevate product management practices across newsrooms worldwide In this episode, we explore CNN's thoughtful approach to AI integration. As Upasana shares her candid insights about identifying which problems are worth solving with AI, creating lasting collaboration between products. product and editorial teams and how she uses tools like ChatGPT to enhance her own product management work. Hi Apasla, welcome to Newsroom Robots. It's such a pleasure to have you here today. Hi Nikita, it is amazing to be here and an honor. Thank you for having me. Vasna, it's my honor. I'm so fascinated by the work you've been doing at CNN. And I really wanted to get into this conversation today. And before we get into chatting all about the fun stuff at AI Understanding really what your role at the intersection of editorial and technology looks like at CNN and I wanted to get a Bit more of an understanding as we kick off with all of this what the team looks like over there focused on AI and technology. And what's the impact of your role where you sit into this team? B I'm imagining it focuses a lot on orchestrating a lot of moving pieces across editorial and technology. So how would you describe the work that you've been doing? Orchestrating a lot of moving pieces. You nailed it. That's it. My official title is Senior Platform PM at CNN and I lead the development and evolution of Stellar, which is our content management platform, it supports two key stakeholder sets. One of them is of course enabling our editorial teams to create, edit, and publish content and program different content experiences across CNN, the website, the mobile app, and everywhere across domestic and international. And then our second stakeholder set, which is an interesting one and we don't really see a lot in news, is Stellar enables our product and engineering teams to build, test, and learn faster so we can release world-class product experiences. for our audiences. So when we say platform, what does that mean? Platform on the technology side includes our CMS, our content API, our digital asset management system, or the DAM, our rendering infrastructure. the distribution platform, the developer platform, the design system, multi-site support, and then all of our third-party integration. So think you know about things like analytics, for example. So like you said, my work involves orchestrating a complex ecosystem of tools, workflows, and components that need to meet this dual demand of speed and reliability, which are my two key metrics of success. A large part of my role is working with my stakeholders and users across the world. journalists who are focused on storytelling, editors who are leading and driving strategy, the developers who are solving technical challenges, and of course our product teams to ensure cross-functional alignment. I would also be remiss if I didn't mention my other role outside of CNN as board chair at the News Product Alliance, which is where I provide strategic oversight and operational direction for the organization. to elevate the discipline of product management across newsrooms, across the world, especially ones that are under-resourced and under under-represented.

And so we work with them to validate requirements, to identify any gaps. to make sure we're on the same page about the problems that we're solving and to make sure that the execution is flawless. So there are core teams and lots of squads and each team has a product lead, a tech lead, and a design lead. I would say our current staff of product technology and design is about three fifty to four hundred people. And I think the way you've described it as a tech company and you're focusing a lot on speed and reliability. So how are you with all of the platforms that you're managing Thinking about which areas really hold a lot of promise for editorial efficiency. Yes, and keeping that speed and reliability in mind. I think the one area that has the most immense promise is workflow orchestration and optimization. This is automating mundane tasks, repetitive tasks, so journalists can focus on creativity, strategy, reporting. And the other key area outside of like workflow orchestration and optimization, I think, is is data integration. How can we integrate data directly into the editorial workflow? What are other teams working on? What are their content strategies? What are their goals? And all because a lot of it overlaps. I think this is pro AI. especially now because it's shiny object syndrome around it.

chunks.document: 2024-12-27 - Upasna Gautam The Product Manager's Guide to AI in News.txt
chunks.text: But in general, when it comes to product development, we tend to skip over the product discovery process and immediately start making assumptions to create a solution. So I begin by Talking to my users in depth, observing workflows, conducting interviews, identifying the bottlenecks or the blockers, Is this a problem about speed? Is it a problem about scalability? Is it a problem about accuracy? What problem are we trying to solve? And why is that problem a problem in the first place? And From there, it's about evaluating AI's strengths and trade-offs. AI is a like ideal for analyzing patterns at scale. It can accelerate decisions and of course Enabling personalization is everyone's favorite thing today. However, it still requires weighing trade-offs. What are the risks, complexities, unintended consequences? For example, will automating a task save time or introduce potential errors that impact trust? And like what other kind of friction could it potentially introduce? These are things that we're always thinking about. And as any role, especially in a product role, these are the questions you should be asking yourself. And these are the questions I think as a news industry we should be asking ourselves because There is this sometimes blind race to just adopt without understanding the problem that's actually being solved. And to go with that, the third part of the structure, in addition to understanding pain points and evaluating strengths and trade-offs, is What are your key metrics of success? And how do you align this problem and solution with your key metrics of success? I prioritize problems that directly improve my team's core outcomes, my platform's core outcomes, which are speed and accuracy. So if there's a solution that can measurably improve speed while also maintaining trust, while also maintaining accuracy, it's going to move to the top of the list. And also being able to quantify that. Is there a way to quantify how much how much of a factor speed is in it? So all of these things, and I think especially with AI, need to be very thoughtfully thought out and documented as well. And I think the heart of this process is clarity. The best problems to solve In journalism, in AI, anywhere, but especially to solve are those where AI's capabilities amplify what we do best. and ensure that there's measurable impact without compromising the values that are guiding the work or the vision of the product. The way you described it about speed and accuracy as your guiding measures of impact and being able to prioritize based on features that are able to help you reach those metrics of success really shows like it's kind of like the backbone of understanding what is an impactful initiative to get started with. But Of course it's not just all about the tools and building about the tools and adoption is a huge part, getting the buy-in from your stakeholders to even get into AI. Since your your users are journalists and editorial staff. There is a lot of fear and concern around AI. So how do you go about managing that and being able to foster collaboration with them? I think no matter what the dynamic is, but particularly in this one between between product and editorial, that collaboration starts with building trust through transparency and empathy. chunks.text: This is Newsroom Robots, the podcast where we explore the intersection of artificial intelligence and the news industry. He's the head of AI at the JP Politikens Media Group in Denmark and has been working on developing and implementing effective AI systems for news. Since 2020. He's also a member of the AI Advisory Board for WAN IFRA, the World Association of News Publishers, and co-leads major industry initiatives like the Nordic AI Journalism Network. In this episode, Casper discusses one of the most comprehensive AI initiatives in journalism, the Platform Intelligence News Project. He shares how his team built Magna, an AI-powered editorial assistant, and we explored the evolution of AI in news, the challenges of creating scalable AI-driven infrastructure. and what it takes to align AI with journalistic values. So we have a lot of that to go through today. Sure, yes, yes. So first and brief about uh about our media group for the people who might not know us. Uh we are legacy news publishers, so for the past hundred and fifty years or so uh we've been publishing these uh three national titles, Extrabladet, Politik and Unit Boston. chunks.document: 2025-07-15 - Florent Daudens How Open-Source AI Puts Newsrooms Back in the Driver’s Seat.txt
chunks.text: This is Newsroom Robots, the podcast where we explore the intersection of artificial intelligence and the news industry. I'm Nikita Roy, data scientist, media entrepreneur, and one of the many founders currently building their. Ventures at the Harvard Innovation Labs. On the Newsroom Robots, I'm excited to bring you insightful conversations with industry experts about how AI is impacting the way we do journalism. Today I'm excited to bring back a returning guest, Florent Dodens. Last time we spoke, Florent was leading AI integration at Canada's national public broadcaster. Radio Canada as the director of News Gathering. Now he stepped into the tech world as the press lead at Hugging Face. one of the most influential platforms in open source AI. In this episode, Florent and I discuss the growing importance of open source AI, the environmental cost of AI tools the role of AI agents in newsrooms, and even what the future of news apps could look like. Hi Florent, welcome back to Newsroom Robots. Hey, thanks for having me again. Well, last time we spoke on the podcast, you were in a completely different role at Radio Canada and you are leading AI literacy efforts there. And now you're in a completely different role at Hugging Face in the tech industry as the press lead. I am so excited to get into that transition, that move that you've had. But also, what is Hugging Face? Tell us about the company. It has a huge space in the open source AI movement, but I think a lot of people are still. Getting familiar to that and the unique name that it has. Yeah, yeah, it's a quite a fun name. You know, the best way to explain what Hugging Face is is basically it's the community of AI. So We often have this analogy of it's a library or it's the GitHub of AI. Basically we have seven million users And on a daily basis they can make use of more than one point five million models. hundreds of thousands of data sets and also what we call spaces, which are basically you know AI apps. So for example, if you're looking for An easy to use transcription tool that you want to run in your browser without having to install anything on your computer, you can type like transcription tool and you will find The a demo with a whisper large, for example, which is one of the most powerful model to run on your computer and then you'll be able to uh transcribe it. confidentially on your computer but with our infrastructure. So basically it's like um we'd say the the epicenter of open source AI Yeah, and as you said, 1. 5 million models that are out there. And I think a lot of people when they think about AI are only thinking about Chat GPT or Clouds Anthropic or Google's Gemini. But there are so many models that are out there, and I think for people who are not very familiar with open source AI, can you talk about what that ecosystem's like? Yes, absolutely. In fact it's interesting because you know every development of ChatGPT or cloud and so on are based on open source because the R But Overall architecture is basically transformers, so it's a fundamental layer of AI. And Hugging Face has been really transformational in this because we basically adapted Transformers at the beginning of Hugging Phase. Can you describe what Transformers is for our audience as well? Yes, absolutely. So Transformers basically takes care of every technical aspect that you need to be able to converse basically with a model with your own words. So there's a lot going on behind the scene like tokenization and so on. So Transformers tech terror takes care of this And it evolves to have uh what we call for example pipelines.

chunks.document: 2025-07-15 - Florent Daudens How Open-Source AI Puts Newsrooms Back in the Driver’s Seat.txt
chunks.text: So you can have a specific task. Let's say you want to do some image classification So you will call the pipeline from Transformers and you will select the model that you want to use. So for image classification, you can have several thousand of them And you'll be like, oh, for my use test, I think this one is the best one because you know it's uh it's small or it's uh adapted to my language or whatever. And so you select this model and then you can basically you code a model with a pipeline, you have your image, and it will output the result. So it's it makes AI very easy to use and and really simpler that if you need to code everything from scratch. So it's uh it's really powerful in that regard. You know, basically Hugging Face has every big AI developer. For example, OpenAI is he is on the platform with uh Whisper and Clip VIT, Meta is there with uh Llama. Mistral, Deep Seek, and so on. So you have plenty of big labs who are basically hosting and sharing their models with the community. From there, the community will adapt, fine-tune, develop or push further the models. M So for example, for Deepseek, the last time I checked, we had probably two thousand models built on top of DeepSeek R1, which was, you know, a model that uh we we talked a lot about because it was really groundbreaking in terms of the reasoning capacities. And so you have this stack of big models and you also have plenty of smaller but task-oriented models. So for example, if you want to build a tool for retrieving your database. So let's say you are a news company, you have a search engine, but you want to improve it with conversational AI. Well you can plug your database of content to a chatbot basically, but to improve the results you would need to do what we call embedding. So Without going to the technical complexities, it will build a mathematical representation of your database so that the the LLM will be able to the model will be able to retrieve information with more accuracy. And we have a lot of embedding models that you can use. They can be multilingual, they can come in different sizes, so you really had to choose how you want to run this model, etc. So There are plenty of uh specific use cases that you can build with with Pugging Face, basically by choosing the best model. And one important thing is that with open source we really put the emphasis on being able to evaluate the models without the marketing fluff basically. So you are able to have leaderboards who will tell you We were able to perform this evaluation in a transparent and reproducible manner. So for example, for embedding, we have a leaderboard that is widely used and you can compare different embedding models to uh to find the best one. Yeah and with open source AI, everything is basically public, the models, the weights. You can tune it, you can run it locally, unlike the proprietary models that are there on like OpenAI has or Google has, where you can't host it traditionally. So what do you see as the wins for the news industry with regards to open source AI? There are basically three or four main advantages. The first one is really a being able to understand how the model has been built. There is a spectrum of openness, I would say. So You have open weights and you have fully open source models. So fully open source models you can really look under the hood and you have how the model was trained. You have the training code, but also you have the training data.

chunks.document: 2025-07-15 - Florent Daudens How Open-Source AI Puts Newsrooms Back in the Driver’s Seat.txt
chunks.text: So it's really important for news organizations because you know there are lots of questions and issues about Copyrights, where does the data come from? And you also want to understand what kind of bias the model can have. So if you generate outputs for user-facing tools or experiences, you really want to know how the model was built. So with fully open source models you can examine how the model was trained and built. With open weights, it's a little bit different. I would say the main difference is that you don't have access to the training dataset. So you can't retrace or trace where the data comes from, but you are really able to understand several key components of the models and more importantly you can fine-tune the model, you can adapt it for your needs, so you can plug your data set, train the model on it, and it will be better than the out-of-the-box model that uh that was um created by the the original model developer. I think it's also important to um to understand that you know many many interactions or discussions that I have with news publishers when it comes to uh dataset. They're like, oh but we don't want our data being used without our consent. It's really striking because I think the discussion has been so much focused on closed source models having like basically crawled every data that was available without any regards to consent that people are like, Oh, I didn't even think that I could have the the capacity to decide how I want to share my data. But it's foundational. Like on the hub you can really decide what kind of license you want to attach to your data set. So for example, you can decide, oh, this data set uh will be shared with uh a page to uh a license or MIT or or on the opposite a commercial license or not for for commercial use license. So it's really All about giving control to the user on how they want their artifacts, their models, their data sets being used. And then you also have in terms of really like important uh key arguments for open source. I think it's also the ability to build your own pipeline and to be able to analyze at each step what's going on and how to evaluate let's say you have a newer model on the market. Well you're not gonna be you won't have to Som change everything because the model provider decide to do so. So you you basically can decide no no everything's gonna stay the same and and then that's perfect. And you can also decide how you want to build your pipeline. Like do you want to host the models yourself on your infrastructure? Do you want to rely on cloud providers or on inference providers So for example, if you want to quickly be able to analyze the capacity of the model, you can just call an inference provider. and try several different models. Like basically one of the on of the most powerful aspect of open source is you can switch models in one line of code. So it's it's powerful, it it avoids vendor locking. it will give you more freedom of choice. And this is why I think you know one of the key missions of Hugging Face is is basically fighting the concentration of power. We want people to be able to choose how and what they want to use in AI.

chunks.text: I feel like another aspect to that is because you can host it on your own infrastructure it's more private and you have complete control over that. When Deep Sea came out, like I think that's also an important huge win for the open source AI community Because that was not completely open source, but it was an open weights model and you were able to download it on your computer and you suddenly had equivalent to open AI's most powerful model, now open source being able to be in a privacy-first manner running on your laptop. chunks.text: This is Newsroom Robots, the podcast where we explore the intersection of artificial intelligence and the news industry. I'm Nikita Roy, data scientist, media entrepreneur, and one of the many founders currently building their. Ventures at the Harvard Innovation Labs. On the Newsroom Robots, I'm excited to bring you insightful conversations with industry experts about how AI is impacting the way we do journalism. Joining me on the show today is Sarah Bakpoor. She's the co-founder and CEO of Particle, an AI-powered news aggregator. Before launching Particle, Sarah spent more than a decade working on product and engineering at Twitter. In this episode, Sarah takes us inside how Particle works. From its reality check process to catch errors and AI-generated summaries, to its bias meter that surfaces how stories are being covered across the political spectrum. We explore how she's thinking about trust and transparency in AI-powered news, why personalization doesn't have to mean filter bubbles. and how AI could reshape the relationship between publishers, platforms, and audiences. Hi Sarah. Welcome to News from Robots. Hi, Nikita. Thank you for having me. Excited to be here. I'm really excited to understand a lot more about particle AI. been this new AI aggregated news experience that people have been talking about. I have been excited to explore it. And so there's been a lot of questions also that's been on my mind about what this new tool is. how this app works, what it means for journalism. So I'm really excited to get into all of that with you today, Sarah. But before we get into everything, can you just give our listeners and people who might not be familiar with particle AI, what exactly does it do and the founding story of how particle began? Sure. So particle is at the surface a news aggregator that is uh powered by AI. So we create news summaries so that you can get quickly up to speed. And we personalize it for you. And we really want to be the easiest way for you to keep up with the news to get a multi-perspective, personalized, streamlined way of like quickly catching up. with the news. And that might mean that you're just kind of skimming through those things that you are interested in, or it might mean that you want to go really deep on a particular story and In that case, particle acts as a map for you to understand what the basic issues of the story are, what the key points are. and how to go deeper, how to go deep into you know the sources that are reporting on it, ask questions about it, further research, other links, all that kind of stuff. So we really want to meet customers where they are And that might mean you have very little time for news today, or it might mean you have a lot of time for news today, or it's something that you're like really interested in for today. So we show you kind of the most Important information about a story in just a few bullet points and you can personalize the experience by tuning the topics that you're interested in. We just launched in November and so we're really excited by all of the feedback that's been coming in and how people are using the product. And uh yeah, that's the that's the story of Particle. So in terms of how we founded it, so my co-founder Marcel and I, we started a couple of years ago. Chat GPT had just come out And the both of us used to work at Twitter and it was going through an ownership change at the time.

chunks.text: And we were kind of thinking, we got together and and and were kind of thinking like this is an interesting opportunity Because with what we were seeing happening with LLMs, we felt like we were at the precipice of something that was really going to change. And so we thought about what were some of the best things about Twitter, which, you know, is or was, depending on who you are, a place where you could easily get caught up on the news. But one of the things that, you know, I've observed and that Marcella's uh has observed throughout my time at Twitter is Sometimes those conversations are not productive. And those conversations on on a lot of social media platforms, one of the reasons that they're not productive is they're not necessarily starting from the same set of facts. Of course everyone has their own opinions and everyone has different lived experiences, but we're also not always starting from the same set of facts. or the like impact of something that might be so an example that Marcel often uses is you might we might be talking about a bill or you know piece of legislation. And uh it might be like cutting healthcare or some funding in said bill. But if you were to actually look and dig deeper into it and if we were starting from the same set of facts, you might learn that actually the healthcare cut here is like a drop in the bucket and is like not as big of a deal. as the conversation that you might be having online. It might not be proportionate to the heat that's happening in that conversation. Just a like a random example that I'm just kind of making up here. But But the point being, we thought, how do we because it's so hard to get up to date on the facts. And the lens through which we learn about what's going on is either a lot of work Or it's maybe not so much work, but it's very biased or it's very charged and the temperature level is high. So we started kind of from there thinking, how do we just get people to be up to date on the agreed-upon facts from all sides? First and foremost. And that's really what the core particle story is about. Because we look at the sources from all across the political spectrum and we summarize kind of what is agreed upon. What are the facts that are agreed upon from all sides? And then to the extent that there are discrepancies, we do our best in the product to call them out and draw your attention to them and say that, you know Here's how the right is framing something, and here's how the left is framing something. So maybe it's a movie review and uh a movie came out and a lot of people hated it or it was mixed. And you know, we want to try to show the spectrum of how a story is being covered. So that was how particle began. I think from your experience at Twitter, you can see that when things are just put in a very concise format, sometimes nuance is removed. And I think that's one of the things I want to talk specifically about particle is the way in which these summaries are created.

chunks.document: 2025-08-14 - Sara Beykpour The Next Chapter in News Aggregation.txt
chunks.text: As you said, these summaries are of multiple different sources. So how do you ensure that summaries don't flatten the nuance or remove any important points from these stories when you're creating it? So I'll say a couple of things. One is we want to make sure that the summary is reflective of the nuance or any like specific details in the reporting. And one of the ways that we do that is through a process that we call a reality check. And what that does is it's an additional LLM process in our whole pipeline that double checks. every claim made in the summary against the source material to make sure that it's properly representing the source material. And And if you're not familiar with kind of like how LLMs work at a deeper level, you might be like, cool, why don't you just do that the first time? Why do you have to do it in a second step? And the reason is because LLMs are not perfect and you know we do see a percentage of errors or misleading outputs and so doing multiple checks greatly reduces the likelihood that something misleading or incorrect will end up in your final output. LLMs are a lot like humans and it's kind of like you might write something, but you're gonna have someone proofread it or you're gonna have someone check it or double check it. And the more people that you have double checking the work, the more likely it's gonna be error free. So that's one part of how we make sure some of that nuance and like important details are captured. Another thing that we do is we extract important quotes. from primary sources and we show those to you within the context of a story and I think seeing from exactly what someone said from the source of the person who said it adds quite a bit of color to what is otherwise kind of like a very general summary of, you know, what's going on in the story, like this is Trump's actual quote or this is JD Vance's actual quote. I think adds some of that nuance that might otherwise be some of that emotion or some of that passion or whatever that might otherwise be lost in in a generated summary. And so I'm quite curious that that that's one of those features that's helping and reduce that hallucination part, but also in the general the algorithm is kind of giving us the news over here. And being able to analyze the different sort of political biases that might be there on news articles, highlighting the reporting. So we do that for hundreds and hundreds of sources so that we can identify kind of the coverage of of a story and how That story is being presented. Now, before we had this in the app when we were still in private beta and we didn't have this bias meter, The AI summary is only a reflection of the coverage behind it. So we would sometimes see stories that were only being reported from the left or only being reported from the right. And that's fine. That's something that happens. chunks.text: This is Newsroom Robots, the podcast where we explore the intersection of artificial intelligence and the news industry. As a computer scientist, a lawyer, and a philanthropist, Velas brings a rare systems view of how innovation can strengthen democracy and serve the public good. V We also dig into the build was the spy dilemma, ways to cut AI's environmental cost, and discuss why journalism must be a co-creator. not just a consumer of technology. And I think in newsrooms, we can't talk about adopting AI without talking about the ethics of what that means. And there are a lot of difficult questions around that that as leaders are dealing with right now what it means about bias and transparency and misinformation. But at the same time, a lot of this is helping us build and innovate new capabilities. And so that's why I'm so glad to speak with you today because I want to get into all of those difficult questions around ethics and how can we actually translate that into the work that is being done in newsrooms. And one of the main things I want to Dive deep into was actually your three-part framework for ethical AI that you spoke about, which was responsible data, clear boundaries, and transparency as the three things you need to be thinking about. whenever you are advising and talking to leaders. I want to actually understand a bit more starting from there. When we think about all of these abstract commitments and principles around ethics, how do we translate that into actionable principles that we can implement within our newsroom? Well thanks Nikita. That's quite a powerful question to start off our conversation. Let me say a couple of things. I mean, in my entire life and career, I've spent a lot of time engaged in a study of ethics, of formal frameworks and the philosophical and ontological backgrounds. But never because I thought the intellectual journey was itself worth its own time, right? Because rather it reflects directly into the decisions that we are asked to make in times of extreme transformation and change. One of the most fascinating things about what's happening in the world today is we're not building a new form of ethics or morality. Instead, we're asking a much more foundational question. How do we take what we know to be important in what we value?

chunks.document: 2025-10-08 - Vilas Dhar Why the Future of Journalism Is Still Human.txt
chunks.text: in the principles that should guide us and apply them directly to the challenges that we're facing when we have new tools, new mechanisms, maybe even some fundamental new ways that we work together. It should be the thing that guides the very specific business decisions we make, the ways that we implement these technologies. So in that context, you've spoken to kind of this three-part framework. I love frameworks. Um they help me make sense of a complicated world. And I think each of these are particularly relevant and germane to folks who are making these decisions in the newsroom. The first is responsible data. You know, we are moving so quickly into a world where AI is embedded into all kinds of activities from sourcing and actually validating the idea that stories are important to actually reporting them all the way through to how they're distributed. And it strikes me that we're often barreling into trying to put new tools or platforms into place without first ensuring that we have made sure that we have real boundaries around the provenance of data. understanding who's represented in the data sets that are shown in stories. That we understand how the ways in which we've collected the library of materials we use. might unintentionally exclude voices or perspectives. And for newsrooms in particular that I think have to represent the breadth and diversity of the stories we want to tell as a society, it's important that we start right from the very beginning. So that's provenance and responsible use. The second is boundary conditions. We are very much in the, if you'll pardon the term, the wild, wild west of AI. We're seeing lots of folks who are going out and trying to use these tools often because they're provided as a pilot. Or because they're developed in-house on the basis of an intuition. So how do we set bright line rules about AI? There are things that we will definitely never use an AI tool to do. For example, the creation of a synthetic quote, right? Using an unattributed source, using LLMs to generate things that might make a story feel stronger, but violate a foundational precept of journalistic ethics. chunks.text: I think that's one of the big issues of like how do we actually distinguish between all of that? How do you think news organizations need to start to navigate all of these big challenges when they're trying to address that they're upskilling their teams? I think one of the places we can start is at almost a micro level of saying before we begin to think about the very big questions, let's just talk about how AI is being deployed in our own newsroom and our own environment. As I work with organizations, I see these kind of three phases that have been emerging in how AI is used. The first is probably what you and I experience every day. People who are just picking up an AI tool and saying, I'm gonna play with this. And often it's used in ways that aren't deeply strategic, but they're kind of like, oh, I was able to use a chat bot to go out and find a piece of information I was looking for, and it was much more efficient than going to a web search. Or hey, I began to use this to draft an internal message, a Slack message, a WhatsApp, whatever it is, right? And I think that's really a critical element that's actually widely accessible. It often doesn't even have to happen inside of a corporate environment. People can access these tools on their own and figure out how to use them. But going there from there to the next level is really the critical element. One where we begin to ask the question, How does AI align to the purpose of what we're trying to do? And how do we begin to use the tools in custom ways that bring together two elements? One, the broad generalized universal capacity of some of these big AI tools. A chatbot that knows a little bit of everything. And pairing it with the very specific domain knowledge that you have as an expert in what you do, as a reporter, a journalist who has that particular nose for a particular story. That has developed a capacity to go out and say, I know where to find this data in places that nobody knows to look, that understands how to use incredibly complex bureaucratic processes like FOIA and other things, right? And where that individual or that team is empowered to say, because we know what we do so well, we have a unique perspective on how we can integrate AI into that work. Can we use AI to streamline the bureaucratic process of a FOIA request? Can we use it to aggregate data sets that we've brought together because of Really kind of pounding the streets and doing the investigative journalism, but now we have new analytic tools that let us extract interesting data. In order to do that There's a kind of two-part piece to it. One is individuals that are gonna drive this because they kind of know what they need to do and they're thinking about how I can use it. And the organization's responsibility to create an enabling environment for that. So for example To say if a reporter says, I want to use X, Y, and Z tool to look at highly sensitive data that includes information about vulnerable people. An organization's response to say that AI system hasn't cleared our our thresholds doesn't really work. Because I think, let's admit it, those enterprising journalists are gonna say, well, I'm just gonna do it outside of the environment. And that creates ethical risks around data leakage and vulnerability. And so organizations are going to need to iterate and evolve much faster than they've ever had to before on their technology policies.

chunks.document: 2025-10-08 - Vilas Dhar Why the Future of Journalism Is Still Human.txt
chunks.text: But also on their capacity to evaluate these new and incoming tools to ask questions about what they need to do to secure them. And then to intentionally create things like sandbox environments that are test beds where journalists can go out and try and play with these tools without creating the risk of new vulnerabilities. So that's kind of the phase two approach, and that's where organizations need to very quickly develop a new muscle and capacity to create ecosystems of AI exploration so that those innovators in the field can do what they need to. And there's a third phase to this work, which is, you know, if we started from okay, we're playing around with tools to just get to know them, we're now using the tools that exist to do what we want to do. Well, maybe the kind of like aspiring plateau here is to get to phase three. where we're actually saying let's stop being consumers of commercial AI tools and build the tools we need in order to do our work effectively. And something really magical happens in that space, that paradigm shift from we'll go see what a vendor will offer to we will build what we need by building our own capacity to create data and AI tools. And we've seen some really amazing examples of that with many of the newsrooms that are in the nonprofit space. Tools like muck rock, tools like the ones that are coming out of various journalistic associations. Even a tool that we built called JournaPilot, a team uh that inside of my organization that created a tool that allows journalistic organizations to repurpose really high-quality investigative journalism. When we get to the point where, let me be somewhat diplomatic about this, newsrooms that organizations that have typically not been first digital adopters. But now have an opportunity to restructure how they do business to change the economics of their work and the quality of their reporting. It requires boards and executive teams to really reassess just where they want to play in the cycle of digital adoption. And for those organizations that say we will be first adopters, we will go out and innovate and create, I think there's an incredible opportunity ahead. I completely agree with you and the way in which you're talking about it, the technology is not just happening to us. We can play a part in that technology and shaping it and in developing it. And if anything, I would say that the cost of at least getting to prototype to imagine a tool and be building out has decreased. significantly than what it has before. But it also brings a lot of questions because I this debate in the industry happens a lot of like whether we buy or build a tool. And what you just spoke about brought this question up again for me. When we're thinking about whether we just go to a vendor and get something, versus a lot of news companies currently say that we are a media house and we are don't have the technological, the technical capacity to focus on build out something, how should we start thinking about even the ethical risks of whether is it a good thing for us to go and buy a tool right now versus build a tool? What sort of framing do you think we should be thinking about in all of that? Well let me tell you, Nikita, I operate in a sector, the sector of philanthropy and institutional foundations, that has been even further behind in the digital adoption curve than some of the other sectors we've talked about today, right?

chunks.document: 2025-10-08 - Vilas Dhar Why the Future of Journalism Is Still Human.txt
chunks.text: And I have to tell you, I have a real challenge with this idea that executives sometimes because of comfort, sometimes because of inertia, say, we are X and we are not a technology organization. And I contrast it to the conversations that I have with any leader in the private sector who says, you know, 20 years ago we realized that was a false dichotomy. There was no way we could be successful in whatever our industry sector is without also becoming a technology organization. There's something profound in that kind of adoption of identity or the response to it, right? That we still feel I think many organizations that are built on a sense of ideological purity or There are many other drivers to it. Say we can exist as what we are even as the world transforms around us. And I have to tell you, I just I think that is not a particularly good way to approach the world. Let me be much more pointed. I don't think there is a way to be a media organization in this world today without also being a technology organization. And that doesn't have to put values into conflict. In fact, in many ways, if we think of it as an enabler and an accelerant, It is the thing that lets us better achieve our missions, and we've seen that, right? We've seen it in the shift of many media organizations to digital first. in their ability to talk to many, many more consumers, to be able to tell more compelling stories because they have the ability to process much larger data sets, the ability to identify patterns and do reporting about the human consequences of policymaking because they have a digital infrastructure that supports it. Now the big question for us becomes is AI a continuation of digital adoption? Something that has been painful and sometimes quite slow, but has also resulted in some really amazing advancements. Or is there something fundamentally different about AI? And without going too far afield, the one thing I might say to you, Nikita, is what I just observe over and over again is even if we leave aside the qualitative differences What is happening is an entirely different time scale of transformation associated with AI. That what might originally have been a 10-year or 20-year transformation journey is now 18 months. And that creates a real responsibility for leaders and for innovators to take on what sometimes feels quite frictional. It feels uncomfortable. It feels dangerous. And yet in order to succeed, I think that people have to take that head on. Completely agree. And I think one of the big things that you're saying is one of the Challenges for us is maybe also to rethink what our purpose is in this world and how we are constructing. Media is no longer just a silo on its own, something that people get. It's so fundamentally intertwined with the technology. And if we want to be able to have a leading role in that, it's not just about understanding what the technology is, but actually also having a role in being making that and building that for ourselves as well. Yeah. I might just say one thing and I just want to follow on and underscore something you said. One of the other challenges here is the allure of saying we're just gonna bring in a solution that's offered to us. We will implement it. And then we will say we are an AI first organization. As you know, I mean at the Patrick J. McGovern Foundation, we've spent a lot of time and have become one of the prominent kind of supporters in the space of organizations going through this. And one of the cautions that I often provide is to say This isn't about pilots.

chunks.document: 2025-10-08 - Vilas Dhar Why the Future of Journalism Is Still Human.txt
chunks.text: It's not about bringing in an AI technology. It's about doing exactly what you described, which is having a fundamental reckoning with What are we as an organization in a world where many of the assumptions we have are being tested and challenged by the broad-based adoption of AI in the world around us? What's our role in shepherding those core journalistic virtues in a world where attention is becoming fragmented, where people are finding fundamentally new ways of accessing and processing information? We're adversarial and bad actors are using AI in ways that are deeply kind of convoluted and manipulative. It requires an organizational reckoning and a potential for organizational visioning to ask who do we aspire to be in an AI-enabled world? And to me, that's a much more interesting conversation than whether we should pick vendor A or vendor B in order to do X task. Exactly. Bringing that back actually into the newsroom One of the other big dilemmas that's there is related to the environmental cost of just using AI. That has significantly decreased and it's getting better in the past few years as developments are going ahead. But that's one of the biggest tensions I find whenever I go into a newsroom. People are there are the camp of people who are very excited of AI, the people who are not. And a lot of the times it's this reckoning of like How can we use and should we use a tool where, yes, it has maybe clear benefits to journalism, but it has a huge environmental cost? How do we balance this sort of tension within organizations? It's a powerful question that many organizations are tangling with. And while I don't have an answer for you, I think it is important for us to recognize that there's a fast moving kind of space here. And we're in the very early days of AI. We're stuck in a pattern where it feels like the ways that we most effectively use AI. are to go to a very limited set of providers who are incentivized to give us these massive power sucking generalized tools. In fact, we almost fetish it as a society, right? We see these tech CEOs come out and talk about like bigger and better, right? More power, more water, more data, more computers, somehow bigger AI. Sometimes I reckon it to uh I think of it as teenager. It's almost like this idea of like masculinity, like bigger is better. We'll just keep building and building. Okay. But that's not the reality, Nikita, right? Like we're seeing innovators on the edges who are saying things like, you know, we don't need bigger models. We need smaller, more targeted, more intelligent models that actually give us exactly what we need and leave aside everything else. Those models are much more power effective. They're take much less water. They take much less energy to deliver on. The question becomes though: if these big tech companies are really interested in building models that'll Maximize profits that'll serve as many as people as possible that are looking for that kind of least common denominator. Who's incentivized to build AI models exclusively for journalism? A sector that maybe is not big enough to command its own market presence that isn't going to be that profit driver. Well, this is the challenge we've struggled with for the last decade is There's not going to be some set of companies that are going to say we're going to innovate massive foundational changes in AI for journalism unless it comes from within journalism itself. So if we really care about environmental sustainability, in the short term, I think many of the critiques that are leveled are really valid, right?

chunks.document: 2025-10-08 - Vilas Dhar Why the Future of Journalism Is Still Human.txt
chunks.text: Well, sometimes we need to figure out do we need to ask fifteen queries of a system When really what we need to do is do the human work to figure out how to turn those 15 queries into one really good one. Okay, well there's a really easy way there where you immediately begin to cut down your environmental footprint. I think a second thing that we should begin thinking about is why does every organization need to have its own instantiation? What would happen if we could build collaboratives where Many organizations bundled together their data and their models so that we can actually have sharing of a lot of these resources and collectively reduce environmental footprints. Where are we going to invest in building the technological capacity to build these shared models? Who's going to be responsible for it? Is it every organization doing their own little part and all of us just hoping that somehow they work together? Or are we going to go back to a much more human model of shared governance and collective action and say, let's actually band together to build something that works for all of us? I think in many ways, this is really the fun question that we get to is so many of these things about AI. And Nikita, you and I have already done this so many times in this conversation. There are actually very rarely conversations about AI. They're often conversations about behavior and decision making. incentives and motivations about how we actually build new social structures that let us leverage these tools both more effectively and more sustainably. And I think that's the through line insight for everything we've talked about, right? Is rather than trying to focus the conversation in on costs five cents of energy and a bottle of water to do a query. We should be asking, how do we transform our social practices so that we can leverage the ways we use these tools to minimize the queries we need, but get much better outcomes from them? Exactly. And I I like the way in which you actually frame that question and something I'm gonna be taking away with me and use for those conversations a lot. Again, another big Issue with AI is this whole double-edged sword in the information space, which shows misinformation, deep fakes that are getting generated. I mean Google's uh new model, image model is just these AI image models and video models are getting better and better. No one can really distinguish what is true, what is not. That creates a space for misinformation to just spread than ever before. And I'm really thinking about what can we do? What is possible? How do we use AI for good? Because you have a lot of Optimism around AI. But when you think about the ways in which can really go wrong in the information space, how do we use AI so that is actually helping strengthen the truth in our information integrity more? Well, I want to start by telling you I am very rarely optimistic about AI. But I am very optimistic about well-intentioned, values-driven people that are making decisions about how we use AI. And I think we can create a better world. I'm a very hopeful person, but very rarely about the tech itself. But I'm very hopeful about people. But let me answer your question through two lenses. First, maybe a very tactical one of how we actually think about AI and misinformation and deep fakes, right? And there's something that we need to do at the newsroom level, at the organizational level, that we haven't quite nailed yet. And to me, that's thinking about the ecosystem of information and how we are making sure that people can find the things that they know to be true.

chunks.document: 2025-10-08 - Vilas Dhar Why the Future of Journalism Is Still Human.txt
chunks.text: I think there's three elements to that. The first is provenance, right? And we've seen a lot of folks trying to innovate different ways to provide watermarks or tags, things that verify that something you're seeing is a real piece of information versus a deep fake. What we haven't seen is some industry consolidation around agreeing to some common standards and some common benchmarks and some common frames that let us think about provenance not just between one user and one provider, but across the entire ecosystem of information. There's a real opportunity there for us to figure out how do we actually tag something to be true when it is. The second element of it is really thinking about verification models. And here AI presents a real opportunity that I think is under-discussed. When somebody has a piece of information, they're trying to ask the question, is this real or not? Today that's a very difficult process. You might go to social mechanisms like to go and search on Google or to look at social media, but there's no kind of gold standard for saying, hey, is this a real thing or not? One of the things that we've been really excited about is the creation of AI tools that do affirmative verification. So tools like Gigafact, for example, where you can actually submit something and say, is this real or not? And it'll use AI to both test that piece of collateral, but also test the social and information ecosystem around it to be able to come back to you and make a recommendation, say this is real or not. I could envision a world where we actually have real-time fact checker agents that are enabled by AI. that maybe are sitting with you as you are consuming information. They can come in and say, you know, this thing is true and this thing isn't. We're seeing a beginning of that in community focused fact checking, which I think is really important. But I think AI will be really important in order to leverage that capacity at scale. And so if you've got like some sort of provenance and you've got some work around verification, then the third is trust at a partnerships level. We know that media is facing one of its great crises in the form of public trust in media institutions. And we often think of AI as something that is a cause. I think of it as a symptom because people who don't have fundamental trust in these institutions are much more vulnerable to AI-enabled misinformation. So how do we really think about the kinds of verification coalitions, regional trust creation, like coming up with new mechanisms by which people know who to believe and who not to believe. Again, note that this is not a technological question. This is very much a social one and a critical one at the heart of journalism today. But how do we reinvigorate a sense of trust for people in the information they get? so that they're less vulnerable and more resilient to AI-induced vulnerability. Absolutely. And I think a lot of that is really intertwined with I'm thinking it's not just the technology, but us coming back to understanding what it is our role, where are we headed towards. And One thing that I keep coming back to when thinking is from your experience, Ashley, because I think you have this very unique vantage point working across multiple industries through your work. What will be missing in news compared to maybe other industries? What is our blind spot right now when it comes to AI? I think one of the big challenges is if you're thinking about a large-scale commercial implementation of AI. It's sometimes easy to define your quantitative metrics of success. Because it's a limited space, right?

chunks.document: 2025-10-08 - Vilas Dhar Why the Future of Journalism Is Still Human.txt
chunks.text: You think about You think about profits, right? Always an easy one. You think about customer kind of NPS. You think about the traditional framework of how you evaluate a business and you say, how does AI help us get closer? I think that's fine. Sometimes like somewhat boring, but fine, right? But journalism, we have a different intention here. I don't think Most journalistic organizations are saying how do we increase revenue or profitability by using AI? Or at least that's not the first step in the process. Instead, we're thinking about a much more qualitative framework. How do we free up reporters to be able to do higher value work? How do we ensure that we are using AI to create more fact-based veracity in our reporting? All of these things that are actually much more values-driven, but are much less metrics-oriented. And I think that creates both an opportunity and a challenge, right? The opportunity is to say we actually get to be much more expansive in our thinking of how we use AI because we're looking to actually sustain a more fundamental strengthening of our core practice. It's almost like if you ever did martial arts, it's like going back to the very basics and saying how do we build more discipline into our work? How do we build more effectiveness into it? And I think we hope that that translates into viewership and visits and hopefully subscriptions and profitability. Now. That's an opportunity, but the challenge is as you ask the question, what are we missing? Well, what we sometimes maybe are missing is that low-hanging fruit that is actually much more kind of base and crude in our motivations, right? How do we say, let's actually make our editorial process much more streamlined? How do we use AI to make sure that everybody in the newsroom has a value-producing and functional job role? And that we're using AI to automate some of the things that seem less interesting. These are hard decisions, but often they can be the things that propel a broad-based AI literacy comfort and normalization across the organization so that you can do that higher value work. And I think the other aspect to a lot of all of this is just understanding what our new role is going to look like. What does creativity look like in this world? And there's also another question I wanted to ask you because AI is becoming increasingly capable of such creative tasks. So what do you see as the evolving role of human agency when AI is able to create all of these great stories and How do we let it not diminish our creativity? I love this question, Nikita, so much. Just this week I was at a community college in rural New Jersey. the first community college in that state actually. I had this amazing, wide-ranging conversation with several hundred people who asked questions and what was supposed to be a 30-minute talk ended up being much, much longer. It was fascinating. But the most amazing question I got was towards the end of that. It was very similar to the question you just asked. It was somebody who stood up and with real vulnerability and fear asked the question, what's my role going to be? Like where do I have agency if a AI tool can do what I can do? And I'll share with you the answer that I shared with her. AI, unleashed in its current formulation, can do some really wondrous and magical things, right? Particularly in the space of information. It can find patterns, it can source facts. It can even help us do more storytelling, but an AI system cannot understand the vulnerability and pain of somebody who has just lost their house because of a bad discriminatory decision.

chunks.document: 2025-10-08 - Vilas Dhar Why the Future of Journalism Is Still Human.txt
chunks.text: Cannot understand what it feels like to be in the room as your son or daughter gets sentenced by an AI system for something they didn't do. Can't understand empathy or care, responsibility, or love. These are things that are uniquely human in this world. And so I never fear for our agency. Because the things that we do that make us uniquely human, no computer system ever can Now, in that there's both a sadness and a beauty. The sadness is we may find that the things that we used to value about ourselves, that we write well, or that we are able to do a functional task. Might get displaced because the system can do it as well as we can. But maybe it will force us into a societal and an individual reckoning That actually changes what we value about ourselves. Our connection to each other, our hopefulness, our optimism, our imagination, our willingness to go and do and try things that make the world a better place. To me, that seems like actually maybe a positive thing if we could spend some time as a society reorienting what we value in ourselves. I think it's also like a personal reckoning, what our value is that we have to be thinking of at this moment. I want to just go in a bit broader and just understand a bit more for you. What is the North Star right now for the McGovern Foundation? How are you looking to create this human-centered world in the next decade? Well, that's a big question. I'll try to give you a short answer. I want to tell you about a couple of things that give me a lot of excitement and hope. The first is a conviction that for all that we've done over the last fifty years to lionize technological innovation, to put all of these startups and their founders on pedestals. We've not done nearly enough to invest in innovation of our social infrastructure, of our conceptions of justice, of dignity, of equality, and of empathy. And I think these things should happen in lockstep, that as technologies open up new possibilities for us, we go back and ask questions about scarcity. about why it is that we feel okay of living in an economic world where some have so much and so many have so little. If we tie those two things together, we might have lived in a world once where there was only so much to go around And today we say AI will increase productivity 10, 100, 1000 fold. Well, how do we ensure that we're investing in the systems and structures that make sure we participate equitably? So to me, one part of this world that we need to engage in is the institutional imagination that accompanies our technological innovation. How do we reinvigorate our sense of what our economy is built on, of what political participation looks like, of what dignity looks like in this world, and how we ensure that everybody has access to education and opportunity? To me, that's one of the great challenges of the 21st century. And I'm very proud that the institutions we support, our partners across journalism, across healthcare, across climate, across any number of other sectors, are really trying to figure out what this new world might look like for us. So that's one part of it. And the second is an intensely personal one, Nikita, that I get to travel around this country and around this globe meeting with communities that sometimes feel about as far away as they can get from Silicon Valley. They don't believe that AI is being built for them, that they will ever participate or have access to this new world that's being discussed.

chunks.document: 2025-10-08 - Vilas Dhar Why the Future of Journalism Is Still Human.txt
chunks.text: But in our time together, we're often able to find and identify these incredible opportunities for people to say not just that. AI is something that what for a very long time has felt like it's something done to them. And maybe they're beginning to feel like AI is done for them. But I want to get the world to a place where we all believe that AI is being done by us. That we're actually building new forms of participation and engagement. That farming communities can say. . I know what my needs are. I need an AI system that shows me exactly how to plant my seed at a certain depth to take advantage of soil conditions so that my farm can yield five to seven percent more crops a year, so that I can make enough money to send my daughter to school. To work with rural health communities that say, we know that the fancy hospitals in the big cities have an amazing capacity to do certain things. We'll never have the equipment that lets us do that kind of imaging, but maybe an AI solution can help us step over that gap. To work with educational systems and schools that are barely getting access to broadband internet, a whole fundamental problem for another time for us to talk about, but maybe today can use AI to massively step change their ability to deliver curriculum. insight, inspiration, and maybe even wonder for their students using AI. I think we could change the story from a few people innovating for all of us to one where we all get to participate in building the systems of the future. I love that optimistic future. And this actually reminds me back when I was in June at the Aspen McGovern AI forum that was here back in DC in DC. And I heard you speak on stage and I remember feeling so optimistic about the future that we are building. And sometimes a lot of times when you're going in the AI circles, there's a lot of doom as well that is coming about. So I feel like we need a lot more Vilas in the world around us. You're very kind to say that. But let me say this, right? I you know, I used to ride I well, I still ride motorcycles. I used to spend a lot of time on motorcycles and one of these things that I learned from this like Physical process of riding motorcycle is you look where you want to go. And by looking there, your body changes, your motorcycle responds to you, and almost without intention, without you doing anything, you start riding in that direction. Sometimes my value around hope and optimism is not necessarily because I think that is what will inevitably happen, but because we need to be out there being hopeful. If enough of us were, I think we could change the trajectory of where we're heading. Yeah, we can. And it's not something that's gonna be done to us. We can be a part of that. And I'm very excited for the future that you are painting over there. Vilas, I wanna end with just A fan favorite question, because I'm curious, how are you using AI in your personal life? Oh, I use it in all kinds of ways. I'll tell you two ways that I just absolutely love. One is I love to think about how our most unexpected sources of innovation exist in the world. For me, that's often my nieces and nephews. You know, these are kids who have grown up in the start of an AI era, and they often are thinking about ways to use these tools that are so different from the way that my kind of stodgy old like executive friends are using them.

chunks.document: 2025-10-08 - Vilas Dhar Why the Future of Journalism Is Still Human.txt
chunks.text: I'll give you an example, right? We had a chance recently to sit together on a holiday weekend and you know everybody was on their tablets or their phones and everybody was doing their own thing. And at one point we all looked at each other, my nieces, my nephews, my wife, me, sitting on a couch and said, Hey, let's do something together. And they said, Well, you tell us a story. And I said, Well, let's tell ourselves a story. And I logged them into one of the AI systems. I said, what kind of story do you want to tell? And over the course of a couple of hours, we told a whole family narrative, a fairy tale of princesses and forests and castles. That was guided by them and their imagination, but was supported and buttressed by these AI systems that built these magical worlds. where it was never out of our control, but it still allowed us to expand our imagination. It was beautiful. If you've never done this, find a young person, sit with them and say, let's tell a story together. It's a beautiful experience. So that's the fun stuff. And then let me tell you, I've been spending a lot of time with teachers and professors at schools and colleges as we just think about what this might mean for pedagogy. And too often, I think everybody assumes the use of AI to do an assignment, to write a paper, to whatever it is, is always going to be a passive experience. So you kind of give it the assignment, it writes it. But here's an unlock that I think people should really keep in mind. I am among these many kind of random things I've done in my life. I'm also a lawyer and I've taught in law schools. I'm a big fan of the Socratic method. And so what I will often do is if I'm working with an AI chatbot, I'll say, I don't want you to create anything, and I don't want you to introduce anything new. I just want you to ask me a lot of questions. I want you to ask me questions that force me to tangle with the weakness of my argument or what I have been too limited in my imagination around. Ask me the questions that force me to expand my thinking and to be better than. It's an amazing way to reconceptualize your relationship with an AI system where the AI isn't the creator or the source of knowledge. But rather as the interlocutor that lets you go out and expand your own possibility. Exactly. And it's using the tool to make you better in a way, rather than letting it do the work for you and getting your insights over there. I have to go and find a young person and do a story with them. That's on my to-do list for this weekend. And remember that young is never a matter of age. Young is a matter of somebody who is young in imagination, in the ability to kind of think about what this might be. And it might be that we find it within ourselves. Yeah. I'm gonna help get AI to help me write a story. That's gonna be the next thing as well. Well Bilas, it has been such an insightful conversation. And thank you so much for taking the time to join me and talk about all of these really difficult ethical questions that we are dealing with in news and sharing your insights. chunks.text: This is Newsroom Robots, the podcast where we explore the intersection of artificial intelligence and the news industry. On the Newsroom Robots, I'm excited to bring you insightful conversations with industry experts about how AI is impacting the way we do journalism. This episode of Newsroom Robots is supported by the Lenfest Institute for Journalism. The Lenfest Institute is a non-profit Dedicated to advancing the business of local news through efforts like the Lenfest AI Collaborative and Fellowship Program. The collaborative works in partnership with OpenAI and Microsoft to empower publishers to explore the use of AI for news business sustainability. Learn more at lenfestinstitute. org. Joining me today are Jim Friedlick, the Executive Director and CEO of the Lentfest Institute, David Shivers, the lead advisor for the Lenfest AI Collaborative. and Matt Bodgie, the Chief Technology and Product Officer at the Philadelphia Enquirer. In this episode, we talk about how the Lenfest AI Collaborative got started why this co development model looks very different from past platform partnerships, and what changes when newsrooms have dedicated AI engineers working alongside reporters, editors, and business teams. We also learn how the Philadelphia Inquirer built Dewey, an archive search system designed to help reporters with their research. This is a conversation about what it takes to move from AI curiosity to real capability in a newsroom. Like how do you staff for it? How you govern it and how you train your people to actually use these tools well. Hi Jim, Matt, and David. Welcome to Newsroom Robots. It's so great to have you here today. Thank you. Thank you so much. Well, I've been excited for this conversation because I've seen a lot about the projects that have been happening with the Lenfist AI Collaborative and especially think this is like an ambitious AI collaboration with A big funder, 10 major newsrooms, and two of the biggest AI companies with OpenAI and Microsoft. And so I'm very curious to learn a lot more about how this program got started What's going on and how especially newsrooms are being supported in their AI journey now? And especially to hear from you, Matt, from the Philadelphia Inquirer. What does that look like and how has how is AI being evolved and integrated within the newsroom? So I'd love to, before we get into all of that, I want to start off with just hearing from you, Jim. an overview of what this program is uh doing and especially how did it all get started. It started by accident. I met Tom Rubin a senior open AI executive in charge of their partnerships and their IP at an industry event. We got talking and I asked his background. He was a New York Times copyboy before going to law school and then going on to Microsoft and then OpenAI.
